<a name="6a00b95f-c22a-4497-b219-d70294d36a88"><h2 class="Heading2">1.2 AD Sites &amp; Replication</h2></a><div>The following section provides an overview of the Active Directory site topology, site links, replication connections, and inter-site transport configuration.</div><br /><a name="038c6fc2-3df6-41fa-9097-8ca7ced88a5e"><h3 class="Heading3">1.2.1 Replication</h3></a><div>Replication is the process by which Active Directory objects are transferred and synchronized between domain controllers within the domain and forest, ensuring consistency across the infrastructure.</div><br /><div>The following section provides detailed information about Active Directory replication and its associated relationships.</div></div><div><br /><div class="Footer">Page 9</div></div></div></div><div class="portrait"><div class="Normal" style="padding-top: 5.92rem; padding-left: 5.92rem; padding-bottom: 5.92rem; padding-right: 5.92rem;">

<div><span style="font-weight: bold; text-decoration: underline;">Health Check:</span></div><ol style="list-style-type:decimal;">
  <li>It is a good practice to establish well-defined descriptions. This helps to speed up the fault identification process and enables better documentation of the environment.</li>
  <li>Ensure Sites have an associated subnet. If subnets are not associated with AD Sites, users might choose a remote domain controller for authentication, which could result in excessive use of remote domain controllers.</li>
  <li>It is important to ensure that each site has at least one assigned domain controller. Missing domain controllers can lead to authentication delays and potential service disruptions for users in the site.</li>
</ol>

<div style="min-height: 247mm" ><div><div class="Header">Microsoft Active Directory As Built Report - v1.0</div><br /></div><a name="0fd59e44-3ad2-47b1-b62a-f863e7dd8195"><h4 class="Heading4">1.2.1.5 Inter-Site Transports</h4></a><div>Site links in Active Directory represent the inter-site connectivity and method used to transfer replication traffic. There are two transport protocols that can be used for replication via site links. The default protocol used in site link is IP, and it performs synchronous replication between available domain controllers. The SMTP method can be used when the link between sites is not reliable.</div><br /><div style="word-break: break-word; overflow-wrap: anywhere; "><table class="tabledefault" style="width:100%; table-layout: fixed;"><colgroup><col style="max-width:34%; min-width:34%; width:34%" /><col style="max-width:33%; min-width:33%; width:33%" /><col style="max-width:33%; min-width:33%; width:33%" /></colgroup>
<thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Bridge All Site Links</th><th>Ignore Schedules</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td>IP</td><td>Yes</td><td>No</td></tr>
<tr><td>SMTP</td><td>Yes</td><td>Yes</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="Caption">Table 12 - Inter-Site Transports - PHARMAX.LOCAL</div><br /></div>
<a name="b7b525b5-41d1-499e-a4e3-fb5a7edaf058"><h5 class="Heading4">1.2.1.5.1 IP</h5></a><a name="367bdc96-3343-4d71-942b-e9baa006ed6d"><h6 class="Heading5">1.2.1.5.1.1 Site Links</h6></a><div style="word-break: break-word; overflow-wrap: anywhere; "><table class="tabledefault" style="width:100%; table-layout: fixed;"><colgroup><col style="max-width:40%; min-width:40%; width:40%" /><col style="max-width:60%; min-width:60%; width:60%" /></colgroup>
<tbody>
<tr><th>Site Link Name</th><td>Pharmax-to-Naguabo</td></tr>
<tr><th>Cost</th><td>100</td></tr>
<tr><th>Replication Frequency</th><td>15 min</td></tr>
<tr><th>Transport Protocol</th><td>IP</td></tr>
<tr><th>Options</th><td>(1) Change Notification is Enabled with Compression</td></tr>
<tr><th>Sites</th><td>Naguabo; SanJuan</td></tr>
<tr><th>Protected From Accidental Deletion</th><td style="font-family: 'Segoe Ui'; font-size: 0.83rem; text-align: left; font-weight: normal; color: #565656; background-color: #fff4c7;">No</td></tr>
<tr><th>Description</th><td style="font-family: 'Segoe Ui'; font-size: 0.83rem; text-align: left; font-weight: normal; color: #565656; background-color: #fff4c7;">--</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="Caption">Table 13 - Site Links - Pharmax-to-Naguabo</div><br /></div>
<div><span style="font-weight: bold; text-decoration: underline;">Health Check:</span></div><br /><div><span style="font-weight: bold;">Best Practice: </span>It is a good practice to establish well-defined descriptions. This helps to speed up the fault identification process and enables better documentation of the environment.</div><br /><div><span style="font-weight: bold;">Best Practice: </span>If the Site Links in your Active Directory are not protected from accidental deletion, your environment may experience disruptions caused by accidental bulk deletion of objects.</div><br /><div style="word-break: break-word; overflow-wrap: anywhere; "><table class="tabledefault" style="width:100%; table-layout: fixed;"><colgroup><col style="max-width:40%; min-width:40%; width:40%" /><col style="max-width:60%; min-width:60%; width:60%" /></colgroup>
<tbody>
<tr><th>Site Link Name</th><td>Pharmax-to-Cayey</td></tr>
<tr><th>Cost</th><td>100</td></tr>

<div><span style="font-weight: bold; text-decoration: underline;">Health Check:</span></div><br /><div><span style="font-weight: bold;">Best Practice: </span>SYSVOL is a special directory that resides on each domain controller (DC) within a domain. The directory comprises folders that store Group Policy objects (GPOs) and logon scripts that clients need to access and synchronize between DCs. For these logon scripts and GPOs to function properly, SYSVOL should be replicated accurately and rapidly throughout the domain. Ensure that proper SYSVOL replication is in place to ensure identical GPO/SYSVOL content for the domain controller across all Active Directory domains.</div><br /><a name="e6488850-c3fc-4133-bd64-c6db85d9ad0f"><h2 class="Heading2">1.3 Infrastructure Services</h2></a><div>The following section provides an overview of infrastructure services registered in Active Directory, including Exchange, MECM/SCCM, and DHCP server information.</div><br /><a name="07ce5869-11b3-45d0-b75e-d555b5adef8c"><h3 class="Heading3">1.3.1 Exchange Infrastructure</h3></a><div>The following section provides an overview of the Microsoft Exchange Server infrastructure registered in Active Directory, including server names, roles, and version information.</div><br /><a name="09658448-ffc8-4944-aa90-16a4da4588f4"><h1 class="NOTOCHeading4">EX16-SERVER-01V</h1></a><div style="word-break: break-word; overflow-wrap: anywhere; "><table class="tabledefault" style="width:100%; table-layout: fixed;"><colgroup><col style="max-width:40%; min-width:40%; width:40%" /><col style="max-width:60%; min-width:60%; width:60%" /></colgroup>

<a name="d9c2619c-dbf0-4477-b80f-da83a3f8c5a9"><h3 class="Heading3">2.1.1 FSMO Roles</h3></a><div style="word-break: break-word; overflow-wrap: anywhere; "><table class="tabledefault" style="width:100%; table-layout: fixed;"><colgroup><col style="max-width:40%; min-width:40%; width:40%" /><col style="max-width:60%; min-width:60%; width:60%" /></colgroup>

<div><span style="font-weight: bold; text-decoration: underline;">Health Check:</span></div><br /><div><span style="font-weight: bold;">Best Practice: </span>The infrastructure master role in the domain PHARMAX.LOCAL should be held by a domain controller that is not a global catalog server. The infrastructure master is responsible for updating references from objects in its domain to objects in other domains. If the infrastructure master runs on a global catalog server, it will not function properly because the global catalog holds a partial replica of every object in the forest, and it will not update the references. This issue does not affect forests that have a single domain.</div><br /><div><span style="font-weight: bold;">Reference: </span>http://go.microsoft.com/fwlink/?LinkId=168841</div><a name="dd94003c-614f-48cb-ab9a-a3159fd22c7e"><h3 class="Heading3">2.1.2 Domain and Trusts</h3></a><a name="f88cab44-eaaa-4a06-84a4-7691da768d16"><h1 class="NOTOCHeading4">acad.pharmax.local Trust Details</h1></a><div style="word-break: break-word; overflow-wrap: anywhere; "><table class="tabledefault" style="width:100%; table-layout: fixed;"><colgroup><col style="max-width:40%; min-width:40%; width:40%" /><col style="max-width:60%; min-width:60%; width:60%" /></colgroup>

<div><span style="font-weight: bold; text-decoration: underline;">Health Check:</span></div><br /><div><span style="font-weight: bold;">Best Practice: </span>Ensure that AES Kerberos encryption is enabled on all Active Directory trusts. RC4 encryption is considered weak and vulnerable to various attacks. Enabling AES encryption on trusts enhances Kerberos security and aligns with modern security standards. Reference: https://techcommunity.microsoft.com/t5/itops-talk-blog/tough-questions-answered-can-i-disable-rc4-etype-for-kerberos-on/ba-p/382718</div></div><div><br /><div class="Footer">Page 13</div></div></div></div><div class="portrait"><div class="Normal" style="padding-top: 5.92rem; padding-left: 5.92rem; padding-bottom: 5.92rem; padding-right: 5.92rem;">

<div><span style="font-weight: bold; text-decoration: underline;">Health Check:</span></div><br /><div><span style="font-weight: bold;">Security Best Practice:</span></div><br /><div>**Accounts with passwords set to never expire were found in the environment. Ensure there are no accounts with weak security postures. Accounts with passwords that never expire can pose a significant security risk as they may not be updated regularly. It is recommended to enforce password expiration policies to enhance security.</div><br /><div>*Regularly check for and remove inactive privileged user accounts in Active Directory. Inactive accounts can pose a security risk as they may be exploited by malicious actors. Ensuring that only active and necessary accounts have privileged access helps maintain a secure environment.</div><a name="62486e07-868f-4552-9ad8-964e9025d65f"><h1 class="NOTOCHeading4">Enterprise Admins (1 Members)</h1></a><div style="word-break: break-word; overflow-wrap: anywhere; "><table class="tabledefault" style="width:100%; table-layout: fixed;"><colgroup><col style="max-width:50%; min-width:50%; width:50%" /><col style="max-width:20%; min-width:20%; width:20%" /><col style="max-width:15%; min-width:15%; width:15%" /><col style="max-width:15%; min-width:15%; width:15%" /></colgroup>

<div><span style="font-weight: bold; text-decoration: underline;">Health Check:</span></div><br /><div><span style="font-weight: bold;">Security Best Practice:</span></div><br /><div>Unless an account is performing specific tasks that require those highly elevated permissions, every account should be removed from the Enterprise Admins (EA) group. A side benefit of having an empty Enterprise Admins group is that it adds just enough friction to ensure that enterprise-wide changes requiring Enterprise Admin rights are done purposefully and methodically.</div><br /><div>**Accounts with passwords set to never expire were found in the environment. <div><span style="font-weight: bold; text-decoration: underline;">Health Check:</span></div><br /><div><span style="font-weight: bold;">Security Best Practice:</span></div><br /><div>The Schema Admins group is a privileged group in a forest root domain. Members of the Schema Admins group can make changes to the schema, which is the framework for the Active Directory forest. Changes to the schema are not frequently required. This group only contains the Built-in Administrator account by default. Additional accounts must only be added when changes to the schema are necessary and then must be removed.</div><br /><div>**Accounts with passwords set to never expire were found in the environment. Ensure there are no accounts with weak security postures. Accounts with passwords that never expire can pose a significant security risk as they may not be updated regularly. It is recommended to enforce password expiration policies to enhance security.</div><a name="742440a0-561c-42af-bdda-7ee5e35f6710"><h5 class="Heading5">2.1.3.2.2 Empty Groups (Non-Default)</h5></a><div style="word-break: break-word; overflow-wrap: anywhere; "><table class="tabledefault" style="width:100%; table-layout: fixed;"><colgroup><col style="max-width:50%; min-width:50%; width:50%" /><col style="max-width:50%; min-width:50%; width:50%" /></colgroup>

<div><span style="font-weight: bold; text-decoration: underline;">Health Check:</span></div><br /><div><span style="font-weight: bold;">Best Practice: </span>Remove empty or unused Active Directory groups. An empty Active Directory security group creates two significant problems. First, it adds unnecessary clutter and makes Active Directory administration more difficult, even when paired with user-friendly Active Directory tools. More critically, empty groups represent a security risk to your network, as they can be repurposed or inadvertently granted permissions.</div><a name="131a7bdd-1c99-4b5f-a42e-9018be8ca271"><h5 class="Heading5">2.1.3.2.3 Circular Group Membership</h5></a><div>If an Active Directory (AD) group has another AD group as both its parent and as a child member, you have a circular nested reference.</div><br /><div>Understanding the impact of circular group membership:</div><br /><div>There is no technical reason preventing the use of circular references between AD groups, Active Directory can still calculate and grant access. The main reason that circular references are considered harmful is that they tend to make management more difficult.</div><br /><div style="word-break: break-word; overflow-wrap: anywhere; "><table class="tabledefault" style="width:100%; table-layout: fixed;"><colgroup><col style="max-width:50%; min-width:50%; width:50%" /><col style="max-width:50%; min-width:50%; width:50%" /></colgroup>
<thead><tr><th>Parent Group Name</th><th>Child Group Name</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr style="font-family: 'Segoe Ui'; font-size: 0.83rem; text-align: left; font-weight: normal; color: #565656; background-color: #fff4c7;"><td>AD - SRM Admin Group</td><td>ESX Admins</td></tr>
<tr style="font-family: 'Segoe Ui'; font-size: 0.83rem; text-align: left; font-weight: normal; color: #565656; background-color: #fff4c7;"><td>ESX Admins</td><td>AD - SRM Admin Group</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="Caption">Table 39 - Circular Group Membership - PHARMAX.LOCAL</div><br /></div>
<div><span style="font-weight: bold; text-decoration: underline;">Health Check:</span></div><br /><div><span style="font-weight: bold;">Best Practice: </span>In a well-structured Active Directory, every group will have a single purpose, ideally with people and resources in separate groups and following a clear hierarchy. If the personnel group is a member of the color_printing group and the color_printing group is also a member of the personnel group, then neither group has a single clear purpose, and both groups are now granting two permissions. Circular references are often the cause of unintended privilege escalation.</div><a name="3b509143-b586-4d3a-b38c-376a269a1503"><h5 class="Heading5">2.1.3.2.4 Pre-Windows 2000 Compatible Access Group Membership</h5></a><div>The following section provides information about the members of the Pre-Windows 2000 Compatible Access group.</div><br /><div style="word-break: break-word; overflow-wrap: anywhere; "><table class="tabledefault" style="width:100%; table-layout: fixed;"><colgroup><col style="max-width:40%; min-width:40%; width:40%" /><col style="max-width:60%; min-width:60%; width:60%" /></colgroup>
<thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Distinguished Name</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr style="font-family: 'Segoe Ui'; font-size: 0.83rem; text-align: left; font-weight: normal; color: #565656; background-color: #feddd7;"><td>NT AUTHORITY\Authenticated Users (FOREIGN SECURITY PRINCIPAL)</td><td>CN=S-1-5-11,CN=ForeignSecurityPrincipals,DC=pharmax,DC=local</td></tr>
<tr><td>SERVER-DC-01V (COMPUTER)</td><td>CN=SERVER-DC-01V,OU=Domain Controllers,DC=pharmax,DC=local</td></tr>
<tr><td>SERVER-DC-02V (COMPUTER)</td><td>CN=SERVER-DC-02V,OU=Domain Controllers,DC=pharmax,DC=local</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="Caption">Table 40 - Pre-Windows 2000 Compatible Access - PHARMAX.LOCAL</div><br /></div>
<div><span style="font-weight: bold; text-decoration: underline;">Health Check:</span></div><br /><div><span style="font-weight: bold;">Security Risk: </span>The Pre-Windows 2000 Compatible Access group provides backward compatibility with Windows NT 4.0 and earlier systems. If Authenticated Users or Anonymous Logon are members, it grants read access to all Active Directory objects to any authenticated or unauthenticated user, creating a significant security vulnerability. Review and remove any unnecessary members from this group.</div><a name="fa1405cc-4ff0-4cc8-957d-a994f559e4af"><h4 class="Heading4">2.1.3.3 Computer Objects</h4></a><a name="40ca3b04-8a43-450b-9865-401454718398"><h1 class="NOTOCHeading4">Computers</h1></a><div align="Center">

<div><span style="font-weight: bold; text-decoration: underline;">Health Check:</span></div><br /><div><span style="font-weight: bold;">Security Best Practice: </span>Operating systems that are no longer supported for security updates are not maintained or updated to address vulnerabilities, leaving them open to potential attack. Organizations must transition to a supported operating system to ensure continued support and to improve the organization&#39;s security posture.</div><a name="6bd4bcee-6783-441d-a54e-70955aec8137"><h3 class="Heading3">2.1.4 Account Policies</h3></a><div>The following section provides details about password policies, fine-grained password policies, LAPS configuration, group Managed Service Accounts (gMSA), and Foreign Security Principals within the domain.</div><br /><a name="a4af9c3b-ec1d-4489-b0b4-93c38ff2e093"><h4 class="Heading4">2.1.4.1 Default Domain Password Policy</h4></a><div style="word-break: break-word; overflow-wrap: anywhere; "><table class="tabledefault" style="width:100%; table-layout: fixed;"><colgroup><col style="max-width:40%; min-width:40%; width:40%" /><col style="max-width:60%; min-width:60%; width:60%" /></colgroup>

<tr><th>Lockout Duration</th><td>30 minutes</td></tr>
<tr><th>Lockout Threshold</th><td>5</td></tr>
<tr><th>Lockout Observation Window</th><td>30 minutes</td></tr>
<tr><th>Maximum Password Age</th><td>42 days</td></tr>
<tr><th>Minimum Password Age</th><td>01 days</td></tr>
<tr><th>Minimum Password Length</th><td>7</td></tr>

Inactive accounts can pose a security risk as they may be exploited by malicious actors. Ensuring that only active and necessary accounts exist helps maintain a secure environment and reduces the risk of unauthorized access or privilege escalation.</div><a name="ef46c633-19d0-4d39-bc3b-b56353384fac"><h1 class="NOTOCHeading4">adfsgmsa</h1></a><div style="word-break: break-word; overflow-wrap: anywhere; "><table class="tabledefault" style="width:100%; table-layout: fixed;"><colgroup><col style="max-width:40%; min-width:40%; width:40%" /><col style="max-width:60%; min-width:60%; width:60%" /></colgroup>

<tr><td>PONCE-DC-01V</td><td>OK</td><td>OK</td><td>Non PDC</td><td style="font-family: 'Segoe Ui'; font-size: 0.83rem; text-align: left; font-weight: normal; color: #565656; background-color: #feddd7;">Fail</td><td>OK</td></tr>
<tr><td>SERVER-DC-01V</td><td>OK</td><td>OK</td><td>OK</td><td>OK</td><td>OK</td></tr>
<tr><td>SERVER-DC-02V</td><td>--</td><td>--</td><td>--</td><td>--</td><td>--</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="Caption">Table 82 - SRV Records Status - PHARMAX.LOCAL</div><br /></div>
<div><span style="font-weight: bold; text-decoration: underline;">Health Check:</span></div><br /><div><span style="font-weight: bold;">Best Practice: </span>The SRV record is a Domain Name System (DNS) resource record. It is used to identify computers hosting specific services. SRV resource records are used to locate domain controllers for Active Directory. These records are essential for the proper functioning of Active Directory as they allow clients to locate domain controllers and other critical services within the network. Ensuring that these records are correctly configured and available is crucial for maintaining the health and accessibility of the Active Directory environment.</div><a name="462cfa0e-0808-49a7-b5d4-c7475807938e"><h4 class="Heading4">2.1.5.6 File Shares</h4></a><div>The following table lists non-default file shares detected on Domain Controllers, beyond the standard administrative, NETLOGON, and SYSVOL shares.</div><a name="671f405c-1990-4e78-804f-4a94e50878f4"><h1 class="NOTOCHeading5">SERVER-DC-01V</h1></a><div style="word-break: break-word; overflow-wrap: anywhere; "><table class="tabledefault" style="width:100%; table-layout: fixed;"><colgroup><col style="max-width:34%; min-width:34%; width:34%" /><col style="max-width:33%; min-width:33%; width:33%" /><col style="max-width:33%; min-width:33%; width:33%" /></colgroup>
<thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Path</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr style="font-family: 'Segoe Ui'; font-size: 0.83rem; text-align: left; font-weight: normal; color: #565656; background-color: #fff4c7;"><td>UpdateServicesPackages</td><td>E:\wsus\UpdateServicesPackages</td><td>A network share to be used by client systems for collecting all software packages (usually applications) published on this WSUS system.</td></tr>
<tr style="font-family: 'Segoe Ui'; font-size: 0.83rem; text-align: left; font-weight: normal; color: #565656; background-color: #fff4c7;"><td>VcenterBackup</td><td>F:\VcenterBackup</td><td>--</td></tr>
<tr style="font-family: 'Segoe Ui'; font-size: 0.83rem; text-align: left; font-weight: normal; color: #565656; background-color: #fff4c7;"><td>VeeamConfBackup</td><td>F:\VeeamConfBackup</td><td>--</td></tr>
<tr style="font-family: 'Segoe Ui'; font-size: 0.83rem; text-align: left; font-weight: normal; color: #565656; background-color: #fff4c7;"><td>WsusContent</td><td>E:\wsus\WsusContent</td><td>A network share to be used by Local Publishing to place published content on this WSUS system.</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="Caption">Table 83 - File Shares - SERVER-DC-01V</div><br /></div>
<div><span style="font-weight: bold; text-decoration: underline;">Health Check:</span></div><br /><div><span style="font-weight: bold;">Best Practice: </span>Only NETLOGON, SYSVOL, and the default administrative shares should exist on a Domain Controller. If possible, non-default file shares should be moved to another server, preferably a dedicated file server. This helps to minimize the attack surface and ensures that the Domain Controller is dedicated to its primary role of managing security and authentication within the domain. Additionally, it reduces the risk of performance degradation and potential conflicts that can arise from running multiple services on a single server.</div><a name="025d5f54-e769-464f-8d38-46644b1ef5c6"><h4 class="Heading4">2.1.5.7 Installed Software</h4></a><div>This section provides an overview of third-party and non-default software installations detected on Domain Controllers within the PHARMAX.LOCAL domain.</div><br /><a name="0238e912-91e2-4da1-8c6b-bfc2a439f596"><h1 class="NOTOCHeading5">SERVER-DC-01V</h1></a><div style="word-break: break-word; overflow-wrap: anywhere; "><table class="tabledefault" style="width:100%; table-layout: fixed;"><colgroup><col style="max-width:34%; min-width:34%; width:34%" /><col style="max-width:33%; min-width:33%; width:33%" /><col style="max-width:33%; min-width:33%; width:33%" /></colgroup>
<thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Publisher</th><th>Install Date</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr style="font-family: 'Segoe Ui'; font-size: 0.83rem; text-align: left; font-weight: normal; color: #565656; background-color: #fff4c7;"><td>Dell Data Domain DDBoost SDK</td><td>Veeam Software Group GmbH</td><td>20260317</td></tr>
<tr style="font-family: 'Segoe Ui'; font-size: 0.83rem; text-align: left; font-weight: normal; color: #565656; background-color: #fff4c7;"><td>HPE StoreOnce Catalyst SDK</td><td>Veeam Software Group GmbH</td><td>20260317</td></tr>
<tr style="font-family: 'Segoe Ui'; font-size: 0.83rem; text-align: left; font-weight: normal; color: #565656; background-color: #fff4c7;"><td>OpenSSL v3.0.0 FIPS</td><td>Veeam Software Group GmbH</td><td>20251119</td></tr>

<tr style="font-family: 'Segoe Ui'; font-size: 0.83rem; text-align: left; font-weight: normal; color: #565656; background-color: #fff4c7;"><td>Veeam Guest Interaction Proxy Service</td><td>Veeam Software Group GmbH</td><td>20260317</td></tr>
<tr style="font-family: 'Segoe Ui'; font-size: 0.83rem; text-align: left; font-weight: normal; color: #565656; background-color: #fff4c7;"><td>Veeam Installer Service</td><td>Veeam Software Group GmbH</td><td>--</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="Caption">Table 84 - Installed Software - SERVER-DC-01V</div><br /></div>
<div><span style="font-weight: bold; text-decoration: underline;">Health Check:</span></div><br /><div><span style="font-weight: bold;">Best Practices: </span>Do not run other software or services on a Domain Controller. Running additional software or services on a Domain Controller can introduce security vulnerabilities, increase the attack surface, and potentially degrade the performance of critical domain services. It is recommended to keep Domain Controllers dedicated to their primary role of managing security and authentication within the domain. If additional services are required, consider deploying them on separate, dedicated servers.</div><a name="6080f564-f2e6-4bfb-8746-048917028d03"><h4 class="Heading4">2.1.5.8 Missing Windows Updates</h4></a><div>The following table provides a summary of pending or missing Windows updates detected on Domain Controllers in the PHARMAX.LOCAL domain.</div><br /><a name="4a59e1de-4124-4e0e-9bef-984687daa9bd"><h1 class="NOTOCHeading5">CAROLINA-DC-01V</h1></a><div style="word-break: break-word; overflow-wrap: anywhere; "><table class="tabledefault" style="width:100%; table-layout: fixed;"><colgroup><col style="max-width:40%; min-width:40%; width:40%" /><col style="max-width:60%; min-width:60%; width:60%" /></colgroup>

</tbody></table>
<div class="Caption">Table 85 - Missing Windows Updates - CAROLINA-DC-01V</div><br /></div>
<div><span style="font-weight: bold; text-decoration: underline;">Health Check:</span></div><br /><div><span style="font-weight: bold;">Security Best Practices: </span>It is critical to install security updates to protect your systems from malicious attacks. Regularly applying updates ensures that your systems are safeguarded against newly discovered vulnerabilities. Additionally, installing software updates provides access to new features and improvements, enhancing overall system performance and stability. Neglecting updates can leave your systems exposed to potential threats and exploitation. Therefore, it is in your best interest to maintain an up-to-date environment by promptly installing all recommended updates.</div><a name="41177b94-1f4a-4f43-8c90-6f9c1ff2ae99"><h1 class="NOTOCHeading5">PONCE-DC-01V</h1></a><div style="word-break: break-word; overflow-wrap: anywhere; "><table class="tabledefault" style="width:100%; table-layout: fixed;"><colgroup><col style="max-width:40%; min-width:40%; width:40%" /><col style="max-width:60%; min-width:60%; width:60%" /></colgroup>

</tbody></table>

Regularly applying updates ensures that your systems are safeguarded against newly discovered vulnerabilities. Additionally, installing software updates provides access to new features and improvements, enhancing overall system performance and stability. Neglecting updates can leave your systems exposed to potential threats and exploitation. Therefore, it is in your best interest to maintain an up-to-date environment by promptly installing all recommended updates.</div><a name="c9c49225-9b76-4601-abc9-56c3dfb8e8ee"><h4 class="Heading4">2.1.5.9 Roles</h4></a><div>The following section provides a detailed overview of the installed roles and features on domain controllers in pharmax.local.</div><a name="e60a3033-e954-46f3-9fd8-d825fdba22c9"><h1 class="NOTOCHeading5">CAGUAS-DC-01V</h1></a><div style="word-break: break-word; overflow-wrap: anywhere; "><table class="tabledefault" style="width:100%; table-layout: fixed;"><colgroup><col style="max-width:20%; min-width:20%; width:20%" /><col style="max-width:10%; min-width:10%; width:10%" /><col style="max-width:70%; min-width:70%; width:70%" /></colgroup>
<thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Parent</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td>Active Directory Domain Services</td><td>Role</td><td>Active Directory Domain Services (AD DS) stores information about objects on the network and makes this information available to users and network administrators. AD DS uses domain controllers to give network users access to permitted resources anywhere on the network through a single logon process.</td></tr>
<tr><td>DNS Server</td><td>Role</td><td>Domain Name System (DNS) Server provides name resolution for TCP/IP networks. DNS Server is easier to manage when it is installed on the same server as Active Directory Domain Services. If you select the Active Directory Domain Services role, you can install and configure DNS Server and Active Directory Domain Services to work together.</td></tr>
<tr><td>File and Storage Services</td><td>Role</td><td>File and Storage Services includes services that are always installed, as well as functionality that you can install to help manage file servers and storage.</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="Caption">Table 88 - Roles - CAGUAS-DC-01V</div><br /></div>

<tr style="font-family: 'Segoe Ui'; font-size: 0.83rem; text-align: left; font-weight: normal; color: #565656; background-color: #fff4c7;"><td>Windows Server Update Services (1)</td><td>Role</td><td>Windows Server Update Services allows network administrators to specify the Microsoft updates that should be installed, create separate groups of computers for different sets of updates, and get reports on the compliance levels of the computers and the updates that must be installed.</td></tr>

<div><span style="font-weight: bold; text-decoration: underline;">Health Check:</span></div><br /><div><span style="font-weight: bold;">Best Practices:</span></div><ol style="list-style-type:decimal;">
  <li>Domain Controllers should have limited software and agents installed including roles and services. Non-essential code running on Domain Controllers is a risk to the enterprise Active Directory environment. A Domain Controller should only run required software, services and roles critical to essential operation.</li>
</ol>

<a name="1fd60f93-f4c5-4be2-90b9-a4ede99a4745"><h4 class="Heading4">2.1.5.10 Infrastructure Services</h4></a><div>The following section provides a detailed overview of the status and configuration of infrastructure services on the domain controllers.</div><a name="3b360688-e22c-45e1-8229-48ede1e44c72"><h1 class="NOTOCHeading5">CAGUAS-DC-01V</h1></a><div style="word-break: break-word; overflow-wrap: anywhere; "><table class="tabledefault" style="width:100%; table-layout: fixed;"><colgroup><col style="max-width:40%; min-width:40%; width:40%" /><col style="max-width:40%; min-width:40%; width:40%" /><col style="max-width:20%; min-width:20%; width:20%" /></colgroup>
<thead><tr><th>Display Name</th><th>Short Name</th><th>Status</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td>Active Directory Domain Services</td><td>NTDS</td><td>Running</td></tr>
<tr><td>Active Directory Web Services</td><td>ADWS</td><td>Running</td></tr>
<tr><td>COM+ Event System</td><td>EVENTSYSTEM</td><td>Running</td></tr>
<tr><td>DFS Replication</td><td>DFSR</td><td>Running</td></tr>
<tr><td>DNS Client</td><td>DNSCACHE</td><td>Running</td></tr>
<tr><td>DNS Server</td><td>DNS</td><td>Running</td></tr>
<tr><td>Intersite Messaging</td><td>IsmServ</td><td>Running</td></tr>
<tr><td>Kerberos Key Distribution Center</td><td>Kdc</td><td>Running</td></tr>
<tr><td>NetLogon</td><td>Netlogon</td><td>Running</td></tr>
<tr style="font-family: 'Segoe Ui'; font-size: 0.83rem; text-align: left; font-weight: normal; color: #565656; background-color: #feddd7;"><td>Print Spooler</td><td>Spooler</td><td>Running</td></tr>
<tr><td>Remote Procedure Call (RPC)</td><td>RPCSS</td><td>Running</td></tr>
<tr><td>Security Accounts Manager</td><td>SAMSS</td><td>Running</td></tr>
<tr><td>Windows Time</td><td>W32Time</td><td>Running</td></tr>
<tr><td>WORKSTATION</td><td>LanmanWorkstation</td><td>Running</td></tr>
</tbody></table>

<div><span style="font-weight: bold; text-decoration: underline;">Health Check:</span></div><br /><div><span style="font-weight: bold;">Corrective Actions: </span>The Print Spooler service has known vulnerabilities that can be exploited by attackers to gain unauthorized access or execute malicious code. Disabling this service on Domain Controllers and other critical servers that do not require print services can reduce the attack surface and improve the overall security posture of your Active Directory environment.</div><br /><div><span style="font-weight: bold;">Corrective Actions: </span>Per security best practices, DHCP Server services should run on a dedicated server separate from domain controllers to minimize security risks, reduce resource contention, and ensure optimal performance of both DHCP and Active Directory services.</div><a name="448d182e-d25c-4706-98d7-5c49401a80c6"><h3 class="Heading3">2.1.6 Replication</h3></a><div>The following section provides an overview of Active Directory replication connections and status between domain controllers in this domain.</div><br /><a name="1afa123b-5227-4067-8c48-55166c6d0e68"><h4 class="Heading4">2.1.6.1 Replication Connection</h4></a><div>The following section provides detailed information about each Active Directory replication connection object configured in the domain.</div><br /><a name="093212c4-9f51-4cea-94ea-1679bca6d80d"><h1 class="NOTOCHeading4">Site: SanJuan: From: SERVER-DC-01V To: CAGUAS-DC-01V</h1></a><div style="word-break: break-word; overflow-wrap: anywhere; "><table class="tabledefault" style="width:100%; table-layout: fixed;"><colgroup><col style="max-width:40%; min-width:40%; width:40%" /><col style="max-width:60%; min-width:60%; width:60%" /></colgroup>

<div><span style="font-weight: bold; text-decoration: underline;">Health Check:</span></div><br /><div><span style="font-weight: bold;">Best Practices: </span>Replication failures can lead to object inconsistencies, stale credentials, Group Policy application failures, and authentication issues across the environment. Investigate and resolve any replication errors promptly using tools such as repadmin /showrepl or the Active Directory Replication Status Tool to prevent further divergence between domain controllers.</div><br /><a name="bf87882b-39f3-4dd4-8f7e-b999785771d8"><h3 class="Heading3">2.1.7 Group Policy</h3></a><div>The following section provides an overview of the Group Policy Objects (GPOs) configured and applied within this domain.</div><br /><a name="ade596b4-6a4c-4e04-81dc-7d8ff3fe43b1"><h4 class="Heading4">2.1.7.1 Group Policy Objects</h4></a><div>The following section provides an overview of the Group Policy Objects (GPOs) configured within the PHARMAX.LOCAL domain.</div><br /><a name="c5b24e74-30d2-49ea-a59e-077977f8f81c"><h5 class="Heading5">2.1.7.1.1 GPO Inventory</h5></a><div>The following section provides an overview of all Group Policy Objects configured in the domain, including their status, security filtering, and link count.</div><br /><a name="b14ba436-a572-4065-b1f9-234475200b1f"><h1 class="NOTOCHeading5">Security Policy</h1></a><div style="word-break: break-word; overflow-wrap: anywhere; "><table class="tabledefault" style="width:100%; table-layout: fixed;"><colgroup><col style="max-width:40%; min-width:40%; width:40%" /><col style="max-width:60%; min-width:60%; width:60%" /></colgroup>

These GPOs do not apply any settings and can cause confusion or clutter in the environment.</div><br /><div><span style="font-weight: bold;">Corrective Actions: </span>Ensure unused or unlinked Group Policy Objects (GPOs) are removed from Active Directory. <a name="6fd70644-caee-4a97-b692-df4df1da4c30"><h5 class="Heading5">2.1.7.1.2 GPO Settings</h5></a><div>The following section provides details about Group Policy configuration resources, including WMI filters, the Central Store repository, and scripts attached to GPOs.</div><br /><a name="6f22da76-445e-4498-8eb9-830cb91f6d27"><h1 class="NOTOCHeading6">WMI Filters</h1></a><div style="word-break: break-word; overflow-wrap: anywhere; "><table class="tabledefault" style="width:100%; table-layout: fixed;"><colgroup><col style="max-width:40%; min-width:40%; width:40%" /><col style="max-width:60%; min-width:60%; width:60%" /></colgroup>
<tbody>
<tr><th>Name</th><td>ByIP</td></tr>
<tr><th>Author</th><td>Administrator@pharmax.local</td></tr>
<tr><th>Query</th><td>1;3;10;78;WQL;root\CIMv2;Select * from WIN32_ComputerSystem where TotalPhysicalMemory &gt;= 1073741824<br /><br />;</td></tr>
<tr><th>Description</th><td>Filter by IP</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="Caption">Table 134 - WMI Filter - ByIP</div><br /></div>
<div style="word-break: break-word; overflow-wrap: anywhere; "><table class="tabledefault" style="width:100%; table-layout: fixed;"><colgroup><col style="max-width:40%; min-width:40%; width:40%" /><col style="max-width:60%; min-width:60%; width:60%" /></colgroup>
<tbody>
<tr><th>Name</th><td>ByUser</td></tr>
<tr><th>Author</th><td>Administrator@pharmax.local</td></tr>
<tr><th>Query</th><td>1;3;10;81;WQL;root\CIMv2;Select * from Win32_OperatingSystem where Version like &quot;10.%&quot; and ProductType=&quot;1&quot;;</td></tr>
<tr><th>Description</th><td>User Filter</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="Caption">Table 135 - WMI Filter - ByUser</div><br /></div>
<a name="95ab4be2-1918-4bdc-9cec-bc8c927d60cd"><h1 class="NOTOCHeading6">Central Store Repository</h1></a><div style="word-break: break-word; overflow-wrap: anywhere; "><table class="tabledefault" style="width:100%; table-layout: fixed;"><colgroup><col style="max-width:25%; min-width:25%; width:25%" /><col style="max-width:15%; min-width:15%; width:15%" /><col style="max-width:60%; min-width:60%; width:60%" /></colgroup>
<thead><tr><th>Domain</th><th>Configured</th><th>Central Store Path</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td>PHARMAX</td><td>Yes</td><td>\\pharmax.local\SYSVOL\pharmax.local\Policies\PolicyDefinitions</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="Caption">Table 136 - GPO Central Store - PHARMAX.LOCAL</div><br /></div>

<a name="d130142a-9b2c-4d68-8aa7-5f3928ef2127"><h5 class="Heading5">2.1.7.1.3 GPO Health</h5></a><div>The following section highlights Group Policy Objects that may require attention, including unlinked, empty, enforced, and orphaned GPOs.</div><br /><a name="15f60c57-e9d6-411c-b778-44f91696b058"><h1 class="NOTOCHeading6">Unlinked GPO</h1></a><div style="word-break: break-word; overflow-wrap: anywhere; "><table class="tabledefault" style="width:100%; table-layout: fixed;"><colgroup><col style="max-width:40%; min-width:40%; width:40%" /><col style="max-width:15%; min-width:15%; width:15%" /><col style="max-width:15%; min-width:15%; width:15%" /><col style="max-width:15%; min-width:15%; width:15%" /><col style="max-width:15%; min-width:15%; width:15%" /></colgroup>
<thead><tr><th>GPO Name</th><th>Created</th><th>Modified</th><th>Computer Enabled</th><th>User Enabled</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr style="font-family: 'Segoe Ui'; font-size: 0.83rem; text-align: left; font-weight: normal; color: #565656; background-color: #fff4c7;"><td>Dead Policy</td><td>2021-10-05</td><td>2023-06-20</td><td>No</td><td>No</td></tr>
<tr style="font-family: 'Segoe Ui'; font-size: 0.83rem; text-align: left; font-weight: normal; color: #565656; background-color: #fff4c7;"><td>Deleted GPO in Sysvol</td><td>2023-06-20</td><td>2023-06-20</td><td>Yes</td><td>Yes</td></tr>
<tr style="font-family: 'Segoe Ui'; font-size: 0.83rem; text-align: left; font-weight: normal; color: #565656; background-color: #fff4c7;"><td>No Security Filtering Applied</td><td>2023-06-19</td><td>2023-06-20</td><td>Yes</td><td>Yes</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="Caption">Table 139 - Unlinked GPO - PHARMAX.LOCAL</div><br /></div>
<div><span style="font-weight: bold; text-decoration: underline;">Health Check:</span></div><br /><div><span style="font-weight: bold;">Corrective Actions: </span>Remove unused Group Policy Objects (GPOs) from Active Directory. Unused GPOs can create unnecessary complexity and potential confusion. Regularly review and clean up GPOs to maintain an organized and efficient Active Directory environment.</div><a name="fba470db-6b3a-408a-bad4-e38f480845db"><h1 class="NOTOCHeading6">Empty GPOs</h1></a><div style="word-break: break-word; overflow-wrap: anywhere; "><table class="tabledefault" style="width:100%; table-layout: fixed;"><colgroup><col style="max-width:35%; min-width:35%; width:35%" /><col style="max-width:15%; min-width:15%; width:15%" /><col style="max-width:15%; min-width:15%; width:15%" /><col style="max-width:35%; min-width:35%; width:35%" /></colgroup>
<thead><tr><th>GPO Name</th><th>Created</th><th>Modified</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr style="font-family: 'Segoe Ui'; font-size: 0.83rem; text-align: left; font-weight: normal; color: #565656; background-color: #fff4c7;"><td>Deleted GPO in Sysvol</td><td>2023-06-20</td><td>2023-06-20</td><td>--</td></tr>
<tr style="font-family: 'Segoe Ui'; font-size: 0.83rem; text-align: left; font-weight: normal; color: #565656; background-color: #fff4c7;"><td>Linux-Settings-GPO</td><td>2021-05-23</td><td>2022-02-04</td><td>--</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="Caption">Table 140 - Empty GPO - PHARMAX.LOCAL</div><br /></div>
<div><span style="font-weight: bold; text-decoration: underline;">Health Check:</span></div><br /><div><span style="font-weight: bold;">Corrective Actions: </span>No user or computer parameters are set in this GPO. Remove unused GPOs in Active Directory to reduce clutter and improve manageability.</div><a name="115f8897-3297-4ea9-8269-133652e325b0"><h1 class="NOTOCHeading6">Enforced GPO</h1></a><div style="word-break: break-word; overflow-wrap: anywhere; "><table class="tabledefault" style="width:100%; table-layout: fixed;"><colgroup><col style="max-width:50%; min-width:50%; width:50%" /><col style="max-width:50%; min-width:50%; width:50%" /></colgroup>
<thead><tr><th>GPO Name</th><th>Target</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr style="font-family: 'Segoe Ui'; font-size: 0.83rem; text-align: left; font-weight: normal; color: #565656; background-color: #fff4c7;"><td>Certificate AutoEnrollment</td><td>pharmax.local/</td></tr>
<tr style="font-family: 'Segoe Ui'; font-size: 0.83rem; text-align: left; font-weight: normal; color: #565656; background-color: #fff4c7;"><td>SET - KMS Server</td><td>pharmax.local/</td></tr>
<tr style="font-family: 'Segoe Ui'; font-size: 0.83rem; text-align: left; font-weight: normal; color: #565656; background-color: #fff4c7;"><td>LAPS Configuration</td><td>pharmax.local/Configuration Manager Computers</td></tr>
<tr style="font-family: 'Segoe Ui'; font-size: 0.83rem; text-align: left; font-weight: normal; color: #565656; background-color: #fff4c7;"><td>Linux-Settings-GPO</td><td>pharmax.local/LinuxMachines</td></tr>
<tr style="font-family: 'Segoe Ui'; font-size: 0.83rem; text-align: left; font-weight: normal; color: #565656; background-color: #fff4c7;"><td>VEEAM_Local_Administrators</td><td>pharmax.local/VEEAM Servers</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="Caption">Table 141 - Enforced GPO - PHARMAX.LOCAL</div><br /></div>
<div><span style="font-weight: bold; text-decoration: underline;">Health Check:</span></div><br /><div><span style="font-weight: bold;">Corrective Actions: </span>Review the use of enforcement and blocked policy inheritance in Active Directory. Enforced policies ensure that critical settings are applied consistently across the organization, while blocked policy inheritance can prevent higher-level policies from affecting specific organizational units. Proper use of these settings is essential for maintaining a secure and well-managed environment.</div><a name="4ffc03e6-b733-43f8-93d5-6662bea3e86f"><h1 class="NOTOCHeading6">Orphaned GPO</h1></a><div>The following table summarizes Group Policy Objects (GPOs) that are orphaned or missing either in the Active Directory database or in the SYSVOL directory. Review these entries to identify and remediate inconsistencies between AD and SYSVOL.</div><br /><div style="word-break: break-word; overflow-wrap: anywhere; "><table class="tabledefault" style="width:100%; table-layout: fixed;"><colgroup><col style="max-width:40%; min-width:40%; width:40%" /><col style="max-width:60%; min-width:60%; width:60%" /></colgroup>
<tbody>
<tr><th>Name</th><td>Unknown</td></tr>
<tr><th>Guid</th><td>A8DF92D3-BDAF-479E-8C0C-9D78AAE058E4</td></tr>
<tr><th>AD DN Database</th><td style="font-family: 'Segoe Ui'; font-size: 0.83rem; text-align: left; font-weight: normal; color: #565656; background-color: #fff4c7;">Missing</td></tr>
<tr><th>AD DN Path</th><td style="font-family: 'Segoe Ui'; font-size: 0.83rem; text-align: left; font-weight: normal; color: #565656; background-color: #fff4c7;">CN={A8DF92D3-BDAF-479E-8C0C-9D78AAE058E4},CN=Policies,CN=System,DC=pharmax,DC=local (Missing)</td></tr>
<tr><th>SYSVOL Guid Directory</th><td>Valid</td></tr>
<tr><th>SYSVOL Guid Path</th><td>\\pharmax.local\SYSVOL\pharmax.local\Policies\{A8DF92D3-BDAF-479E-8C0C-9D78AAE058E4} (Valid)</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="Caption">Table 142 - Orphaned GPO - PHARMAX.LOCAL</div><br /></div>
<div><span style="font-weight: bold; text-decoration: underline;">Health Check:</span></div><br /><div><span style="font-weight: bold;">Corrective Actions: </span>Evaluate orphaned Group Policy objects that exist in SYSVOL but not in AD or the Group Policy Management Console (GPMC). These take up space in SYSVOL and consume bandwidth during replication. Ensure that these orphaned objects are reviewed and removed if they are no longer needed to maintain a clean and efficient Active Directory environment.</div><br /><div style="word-break: break-word; overflow-wrap: anywhere; "><table class="tabledefault" style="width:100%; table-layout: fixed;"><colgroup><col style="max-width:40%; min-width:40%; width:40%" /><col style="max-width:60%; min-width:60%; width:60%" /></colgroup>
<tbody>
<tr><th>Name</th><td>Deleted GPO in Sysvol</td></tr>
<tr><th>Guid</th><td>09E68095-8CFC-4174-81ED-AFB52597DD7F</td></tr>
<tr><th>AD DN Database</th><td>Valid</td></tr>
<tr><th>AD DN Path</th><td>CN={09E68095-8CFC-4174-81ED-AFB52597DD7F},CN=Policies,CN=System,DC=pharmax,DC=local (Valid)</td></tr>
<tr><th>SYSVOL Guid Directory</th><td style="font-family: 'Segoe Ui'; font-size: 0.83rem; text-align: left; font-weight: normal; color: #565656; background-color: #fff4c7;">Missing</td></tr>
<tr><th>SYSVOL Guid Path</th><td style="font-family: 'Segoe Ui'; font-size: 0.83rem; text-align: left; font-weight: normal; color: #565656; background-color: #fff4c7;">\\pharmax.local\SYSVOL\pharmax.local\Policies\{09E68095-8CFC-4174-81ED-AFB52597DD7F} (Missing)</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="Caption">Table 143 - Orphaned GPO - PHARMAX.LOCAL</div><br /></div>
<div><span style="font-weight: bold; text-decoration: underline;">Health Check:</span></div><br /><div><span style="font-weight: bold;">Corrective Actions: </span>Evaluate orphaned Group Policy folders and files that exist in AD or the Group Policy Management Console (GPMC) but not in SYSVOL. These take up space in the AD database and consume bandwidth during replication. Ensure that these orphaned objects are reviewed and removed if they are no longer needed to maintain a clean and efficient Active Directory environment.</div><br /><a name="cf5b89df-48cf-456c-b527-f7cbf7b00414"><h3 class="Heading3">2.1.8 Organizational Units</h3></a><div>The following section provides a detailed overview of Active Directory Organizational Units within the domain.</div><br /><div style="word-break: break-word; overflow-wrap: anywhere; "><table class="tabledefault" style="width:100%; table-layout: fixed;"><colgroup><col style="max-width:45%; min-width:45%; width:45%" /><col style="max-width:45%; min-width:45%; width:45%" /><col style="max-width:10%; min-width:10%; width:10%" /></colgroup>

<div><span style="font-weight: bold; text-decoration: underline;">Health Check:</span></div><br /><div><span style="font-weight: bold;">Best Practice: </span>If the Organizational Units (OUs) in your Active Directory are not protected from accidental deletion, your environment can experience disruptions caused by accidental bulk deletion of objects. All OUs in this domain should be protected from accidental deletion.</div><a name="2936ad6b-6249-4842-a738-21ff49e6b373"><h1 class="NOTOCHeading4">GPO Blocked Inheritance</h1></a><div style="word-break: break-word; overflow-wrap: anywhere; "><table class="tabledefault" style="width:100%; table-layout: fixed;"><colgroup><col style="max-width:35%; min-width:35%; width:35%" /><col style="max-width:15%; min-width:15%; width:15%" /><col style="max-width:15%; min-width:15%; width:15%" /><col style="max-width:35%; min-width:35%; width:35%" /></colgroup>
<thead><tr><th>OU Name</th><th>Container Type</th><th>Inheritance Blocked</th><th>Path</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr style="font-family: 'Segoe Ui'; font-size: 0.83rem; text-align: left; font-weight: normal; color: #565656; background-color: #fff4c7;"><td>admin</td><td>OU</td><td>Yes</td><td>pharmax.local/Admin</td></tr>
<tr style="font-family: 'Segoe Ui'; font-size: 0.83rem; text-align: left; font-weight: normal; color: #565656; background-color: #fff4c7;"><td>linuxmachines</td><td>OU</td><td>Yes</td><td>pharmax.local/LinuxMachines</td></tr>
<tr style="font-family: 'Segoe Ui'; font-size: 0.83rem; text-align: left; font-weight: normal; color: #565656; background-color: #fff4c7;"><td>veeam workstations</td><td>OU</td><td>Yes</td><td>pharmax.local/VEEAM WorkStations</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="Caption">Table 145 - Blocked Inheritance GPO - PHARMAX.LOCAL</div><br /></div>
<div><span style="font-weight: bold; text-decoration: underline;">Health Check:</span></div><br /><div><span style="font-weight: bold;">Corrective Actions: </span>Review the use of enforced policies and blocked policy inheritance in Active Directory. Enforced policies ensure that specific Group Policy Objects (GPOs) are applied and cannot be overridden by other GPOs. Blocked policy inheritance prevents GPOs from parent containers from being applied to the Organizational Unit (OU). While these settings can be useful for maintaining strict policy application, they can also lead to unexpected results and complicate troubleshooting. Ensure that the use of these settings aligns with your organization&#39;s policy management strategy and does not inadvertently cause issues.</div><a name="c952fab1-21d3-4393-8593-e3667d637203"><h3 class="Heading3">2.1.9 Active Directory Hardening</h3></a><div>The following section provides an overview of critical Active Directory security hardening settings, including authentication protocols, SMB configurations, and LDAP security enforcement mechanisms.</div><br /><div style="word-break: break-word; overflow-wrap: anywhere; "><table class="tabledefault" style="width:100%; table-layout: fixed;"><colgroup><col style="max-width:40%; min-width:40%; width:40%" /><col style="max-width:60%; min-width:60%; width:60%" /></colgroup>
<tbody>
<tr><th>NTLMv1 configuration</th><td>Send NTLMv2 response only\refuse LM &amp; NTLM</td></tr>
<tr><th>SMBv1 status</th><td style="font-family: 'Segoe Ui'; font-size: 0.83rem; text-align: left; font-weight: normal; color: #565656; background-color: #feddd7;">Installed\Enabled</td></tr>
<tr><th>Enforcing SMB Signing</th><td>Enable</td></tr>
<tr><th>Enforcing LDAP Signing</th><td>Require Signing</td></tr>
<tr><th>Enforcing LDAP Channel Binding</th><td style="font-family: 'Segoe Ui'; font-size: 0.83rem; text-align: left; font-weight: normal; color: #565656; background-color: #fff4c7;">Not Configured/Disabled</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="Caption">Table 146 - Active Directory Hardening - PHARMAX.LOCAL</div><br /></div>
<div><span style="font-weight: bold; text-decoration: underline;">Health Check:</span></div><br /><div><span style="font-weight: bold;">Best Practice: </span>SMBv1 is enabled on this system. SMBv1 is an outdated protocol that is vulnerable to several security issues, including the EternalBlue exploit used in widespread ransomware attacks. Disable or uninstall SMBv1 on all systems; it has been deprecated and replaced by SMBv2 and SMBv3, which offer improved security features.</div><br /><div><span style="font-weight: bold;">Best Practice: </span>LDAP channel binding enforcement is not configured on this domain controller. LDAP channel binding is a security feature that protects against man-in-the-middle attacks by binding the LDAP session to the TLS channel, ensuring the authenticity and integrity of LDAP communications. Configure LDAP channel binding on all domain controllers.</div><br /><a name="a5210fe9-8587-4f4c-9ce3-18721f82cdd5"><h3 class="Heading3">2.1.10 Health Checks</h3></a><a name="faaf52e3-b9f5-42f6-bf30-ba3f72439dbe"><h1 class="NOTOCHeading4">Naming Context Last Backup</h1></a><div>The following section provides the last backup timestamps for each Active Directory naming context (Domain, Configuration, and Schema partitions) in the PHARMAX.LOCAL domain.</div><br /><div style="word-break: break-word; overflow-wrap: anywhere; "><table class="tabledefault" style="width:100%; table-layout: fixed;"><colgroup><col style="max-width:60%; min-width:60%; width:60%" /><col style="max-width:20%; min-width:20%; width:20%" /><col style="max-width:20%; min-width:20%; width:20%" /></colgroup>

<div><span style="font-weight: bold; text-decoration: underline;">Health Check:</span></div><br /><div><span style="font-weight: bold;">Corrective Actions: </span>Ensure there is a recent (&lt;180 days) Active Directory backup. Regular backups are crucial for disaster recovery and maintaining the integrity of your Active Directory environment. Consider setting up automated backup schedules and regularly verifying the backup status to prevent data loss.</div><a name="b11f9d88-4f77-4b72-b612-126de20a5dca"><h1 class="NOTOCHeading4">Sysvol Replication Status</h1></a><div>The following section provides the replication status of the SYSVOL folder for domain PHARMAX.LOCAL.</div><br /><div style="word-break: break-word; overflow-wrap: anywhere; "><table class="tabledefault" style="width:100%; table-layout: fixed;"><colgroup><col style="max-width:20%; min-width:20%; width:20%" /><col style="max-width:16%; min-width:16%; width:16%" /><col style="max-width:16%; min-width:16%; width:16%" /><col style="max-width:16%; min-width:16%; width:16%" /><col style="max-width:16%; min-width:16%; width:16%" /><col style="max-width:16%; min-width:16%; width:16%" /></colgroup>

<div><span style="font-weight: bold; text-decoration: underline;">Health Check:</span></div><br /><div><span style="font-weight: bold;">Corrective Actions: </span>SYSVOL is a special directory that resides on each domain controller (DC) within a domain. The directory comprises folders that store Group Policy objects (GPOs) and logon scripts that clients need to access and synchronize between DCs. For these logon scripts and GPOs to function properly, SYSVOL should be replicated accurately and rapidly throughout the domain. Ensure that proper SYSVOL replication is in place to ensure identical GPO/SYSVOL content for the domain controller across all Active Directory domains.</div><br /><a name="d881d1a9-a1cf-4a0f-a2df-7c2fb97a97b0"><h1 class="NOTOCHeading4">Sysvol Content Status</h1></a><div>The following section provides the SYSVOL health status for domain PHARMAX.LOCAL.</div><br /><div style="word-break: break-word; overflow-wrap: anywhere; "><table class="tabledefault" style="width:100%; table-layout: fixed;"><colgroup><col style="max-width:33%; min-width:33%; width:33%" /><col style="max-width:33%; min-width:33%; width:33%" /><col style="max-width:34%; min-width:34%; width:34%" /></colgroup>

Problematic settings include passwords that never expire, reversible password encryption enabled, Kerberos pre-authentication disabled, Kerberos DES encryption in use, and the presence of SID history. These settings can significantly increase the risk of unauthorized access or privilege escalation.</div><a name="53608214-811b-480a-bad0-15d38d15fe75"><h1 class="NOTOCHeading4">Privileged Users Assessment</h1></a><div>The following section provides a detailed assessment of privileged administrative accounts (user accounts with AdminCount attribute set to 1) within the domain PHARMAX.LOCAL.</div><br /><div style="word-break: break-word; overflow-wrap: anywhere; "><table class="tabledefault" style="width:100%; table-layout: fixed;"><colgroup><col style="max-width:40%; min-width:40%; width:40%" /><col style="max-width:15%; min-width:15%; width:15%" /><col style="max-width:15%; min-width:15%; width:15%" /><col style="max-width:15%; min-width:15%; width:15%" /><col style="max-width:15%; min-width:15%; width:15%" /></colgroup>

<div><span style="font-weight: bold; text-decoration: underline;">Health Check:</span></div><br /><div><span style="font-weight: bold;">Security Best Practice:</span></div><br /><div>* Privileged accounts such as those belonging to any of the Administrators groups must not have configured email.</div><br /><div>** Privileged accounts such as those belonging to any of the administrator groups must not be trusted for delegation. Allowing privileged accounts to be trusted for delegation provides a means for privilege escalation from a compromised system. Delegation of privileged accounts must be prohibited. Reference: <span style="color: #0000ff;">https://www.stigviewer.com/stig/active_directory_domain/2017-12-15/finding/V-36435</span></div><a name="ffe92abe-9649-4cc3-9872-f96d9157fe96"><h1 class="NOTOCHeading4">Inactive Privileged Accounts</h1></a><div>The following section identifies privileged accounts in domain PHARMAX.LOCAL that have remained inactive for over 30 days and have not had their passwords changed in at least 365 days.</div><br /><div style="word-break: break-word; overflow-wrap: anywhere; "><table class="tabledefault" style="width:100%; table-layout: fixed;"><colgroup><col style="max-width:40%; min-width:40%; width:40%" /><col style="max-width:20%; min-width:20%; width:20%" /><col style="max-width:20%; min-width:20%; width:20%" /><col style="max-width:20%; min-width:20%; width:20%" /></colgroup>
<thead><tr><th>Username</th><th>Created</th><th>Password Last Set</th><th>Last Logon Date</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr style="font-family: 'Segoe Ui'; font-size: 0.83rem; text-align: left; font-weight: normal; color: #565656; background-color: #fff4c7;"><td>veeam_admin</td><td>12/13/2019</td><td>12/13/2019</td><td>11/22/2025</td></tr>
<tr style="font-family: 'Segoe Ui'; font-size: 0.83rem; text-align: left; font-weight: normal; color: #565656; background-color: #fff4c7;"><td>svc_SCCM_ClientPush</td><td>9/12/2020</td><td>9/12/2020</td><td>9/14/2020</td></tr>
<tr style="font-family: 'Segoe Ui'; font-size: 0.83rem; text-align: left; font-weight: normal; color: #565656; background-color: #fff4c7;"><td>1227935471SA</td><td>5/28/2023</td><td>5/28/2023</td><td>--</td></tr>
<tr style="font-family: 'Segoe Ui'; font-size: 0.83rem; text-align: left; font-weight: normal; color: #565656; background-color: #fff4c7;"><td>GERARDO_RICE</td><td>5/29/2023</td><td>5/29/2023</td><td>--</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="Caption">Table 153 - Inactive Privileged Accounts - PHARMAX.LOCAL</div><br /></div>
<div><span style="font-weight: bold; text-decoration: underline;">Health Check:</span></div><br /><div><span style="font-weight: bold;">Corrective Actions: </span>Unused or underutilized accounts in highly privileged groups, outside of any break-glass emergency accounts like the default Administrator account, should have their AD Admin privileges removed.</div><a name="b7726658-727b-445f-b92c-10216367b326"><h1 class="NOTOCHeading4">Service Accounts Assessment (Kerberoastable)</h1></a><div>The following section provides an overview of service accounts (user accounts with Service Principal Names) that are potentially vulnerable to Kerberoasting attacks in domain PHARMAX.LOCAL.</div><br /><div style="word-break: break-word; overflow-wrap: anywhere; "><table class="tabledefault" style="width:100%; table-layout: fixed;"><colgroup><col style="max-width:30%; min-width:30%; width:30%" /><col style="max-width:12%; min-width:12%; width:12%" /><col style="max-width:14%; min-width:14%; width:14%" /><col style="max-width:14%; min-width:14%; width:14%" /><col style="max-width:30%; min-width:30%; width:30%" /></colgroup>
<thead><tr><th>Username</th><th>Enabled</th><th>Password Last Set</th><th>Last Logon Date</th><th>Service Principal Name</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td>vcenter</td><td>Yes</td><td>12/13/2019</td><td>12/13/2019</td><td>CIFS/ACAD-DNS-01V</td></tr>
<tr><td>svc_SCCM_ClientPush</td><td>Yes</td><td>9/12/2020</td><td>9/14/2020</td><td>CIFS/VEEAM-HV-01</td></tr>
<tr><td>krbtgt</td><td>No</td><td>6/10/2018</td><td>--</td><td>CIFS/VEEAM-VBR-01V kadmin/changepw</td></tr>
<tr><td>srmrecadmin</td><td>Yes</td><td>10/25/2021</td><td>--</td><td>ftp/VEEAM-EM</td></tr>
<tr><td>jocolon</td><td>Yes</td><td>11/30/2021</td><td>12/22/2043</td><td>HTTP/example.com</td></tr>
<tr><td>horizon-ic</td><td>Yes</td><td>9/14/2025</td><td>10/20/2025</td><td>https/GOOWLPT1000001</td></tr>
<tr style="font-family: 'Segoe Ui'; font-size: 0.83rem; text-align: left; font-weight: normal; color: #565656; background-color: #feddd7;"><td>** GERARDO_RICE</td><td>Yes</td><td>5/29/2023</td><td>--</td><td>POP3/SECWVIR1000255</td></tr>
<tr style="font-family: 'Segoe Ui'; font-size: 0.83rem; text-align: left; font-weight: normal; color: #565656; background-color: #feddd7;"><td>** Administrator</td><td>Yes</td><td>6/10/2018</td><td>12/10/2053</td><td>SCVMM/SCVMM-SVR-01V SCVMM/SCVMM-SVR-01V.pharmax.local VeeamCdpSvc/VEEAM-VBR VeeamCdpSvc/VEEAM-VBR.pharmax.local VeeamCloudConnectSvc/VEEAM-VBR VeeamCloudConnectSvc/VEEAM-VBR.pharmax.local VeeamBackupSvc/VEEAM-VBR VeeamBackupSvc/VEEAM-VBR.pharmax.local VeeamCatalogSvc/VEEAM-VBR VeeamCatalogSvc/VEEAM-VBR.pharmax.local VeeamEnterpriseManagerSvc/VEEAM-EM VeeamEnterpriseManagerSvc/VEEAM-EM.pharmax.local VeeamCatalogSvc/VEEAM-EM VeeamCatalogSvc/VEEAM-EM.pharmax.local</td></tr>
<tr><td>veeam_admin</td><td>Yes</td><td>12/13/2019</td><td>11/22/2025</td><td>VeeamCdpSvc/VEEAM-DRO-01V VeeamCdpSvc/VEEAM-DRO-01V.pharmax.local VeeamCloudConnectSvc/VEEAM-DRO-01V VeeamCloudConnectSvc/VEEAM-DRO-01V.pharmax.local VeeamBackupSvc/VEEAM-DRO-01V VeeamBackupSvc/VEEAM-DRO-01V.pharmax.local VeeamCatalogSvc/VEEAM-DRO-01V VeeamCatalogSvc/VEEAM-DRO-01V.pharmax.local</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="Caption">Table 154 - Service Accounts Assessment (Kerberoastable) - PHARMAX.LOCAL</div><br /></div>
<div><span style="font-weight: bold; text-decoration: underline;">Health Check:</span></div><br /><div><span style="font-weight: bold;">Security Best Practice: </span>** Attackers are most interested in Service Accounts that are members of highly privileged groups like Domain Admins. A quick way to check for this is to enumerate all user accounts with the attribute AdminCount equal to 1. This means an attacker may just ask Active Directory for all user accounts with an SPN and with AdminCount=1. Ensure that there are no privileged accounts that have SPNs assigned to them.</div><a name="09f69594-15ec-4950-be0f-cfa6a0d03f08"><h1 class="NOTOCHeading4">Unconstrained Kerberos Delegation</h1></a><div>The following section identifies systems configured with unconstrained Kerberos delegation, which represents a significant security risk in the domain PHARMAX.LOCAL.</div><br /><div style="word-break: break-word; overflow-wrap: anywhere; "><table class="tabledefault" style="width:100%; table-layout: fixed;"><colgroup><col style="max-width:40%; min-width:40%; width:40%" /><col style="max-width:60%; min-width:60%; width:60%" /></colgroup>
<thead><tr><th>Name</th><th>Distinguished Name</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr style="font-family: 'Segoe Ui'; font-size: 0.83rem; text-align: left; font-weight: normal; color: #565656; background-color: #fff4c7;"><td>HV-SERVER-01V</td><td>CN=HV-SERVER-01V,OU=Member Servers,DC=pharmax,DC=local</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="Caption">Table 155 - Unconstrained Kerberos Delegation - PHARMAX.LOCAL</div><br /></div>
<div><span style="font-weight: bold; text-decoration: underline;">Health Check:</span></div><br /><div><span style="font-weight: bold;">Corrective Actions: </span>Ensure there are no instances of unconstrained Kerberos delegation in Active Directory, as it poses a security risk by allowing any service to impersonate users.</div><a name="999c2c2c-de8a-4c46-bb68-5c5cd1218fd5"><h1 class="NOTOCHeading4">KRBTGT Account Audit</h1></a><div>The following section provides a detailed audit of the KRBTGT account, which is critical for Kerberos ticket-granting services in the domain PHARMAX.LOCAL.</div><br /><div style="word-break: break-word; overflow-wrap: anywhere; "><table class="tabledefault" style="width:100%; table-layout: fixed;"><colgroup><col style="max-width:40%; min-width:40%; width:40%" /><col style="max-width:60%; min-width:60%; width:60%" /></colgroup>
<tbody>
<tr><th>Name</th><td>krbtgt</td></tr>
<tr><th>Created</th><td>06/10/2018 21:00:49</td></tr>
<tr><th>Password Last Set</th><td style="font-family: 'Segoe Ui'; font-size: 0.83rem; text-align: left; font-weight: normal; color: #565656; background-color: #fff4c7;">06/10/2018 21:00:49</td></tr>
<tr><th>Distinguished Name</th><td>CN=krbtgt,CN=Users,DC=pharmax,DC=local</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="Caption">Table 156 - KRBTGT Account Audit - PHARMAX.LOCAL</div><br /></div>
<div><span style="font-weight: bold; text-decoration: underline;">Health Check:</span></div><br /><div><span style="font-weight: bold;">Best Practice: </span>Microsoft recommends resetting the KRBTGT account password at least twice per year (approximately every 180 days) to reduce the risk of Kerberos ticket forgery attacks, such as Golden Ticket attacks. Note that a second reset is required after the maximum domain replication cycle has completed to fully invalidate previously issued tickets.</div><a name="89e85ee5-e593-4dc2-be93-45da3c12806d"><h1 class="NOTOCHeading4">Administrator Account Audit</h1></a><div>The following section provides a detailed audit of the built-in Administrator account, which is a critical privileged account in the domain PHARMAX.LOCAL.</div><br /><div style="word-break: break-word; overflow-wrap: anywhere; "><table class="tabledefault" style="width:100%; table-layout: fixed;"><colgroup><col style="max-width:40%; min-width:40%; width:40%" /><col style="max-width:60%; min-width:60%; width:60%" /></colgroup>
<tbody>
<tr><th>Name</th><td>Administrator</td></tr>
<tr><th>Created</th><td>06/10/2018 21:00:05</td></tr>
<tr><th>Password Last Set</th><td style="font-family: 'Segoe Ui'; font-size: 0.83rem; text-align: left; font-weight: normal; color: #565656; background-color: #fff4c7;">06/10/2018 04:01:50</td></tr>
<tr><th>Last Logon Date</th><td>12/10/2053 19:01:07</td></tr>
<tr><th>Distinguished Name</th><td>CN=Administrator,CN=Users,DC=pharmax,DC=local</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="Caption">Table 157 - Administrator Account Audit - PHARMAX.LOCAL</div><br /></div>
<div><span style="font-weight: bold; text-decoration: underline;">Health Check:</span></div><br /><div><span style="font-weight: bold;">Best Practice: </span>Microsoft recommends using a unique, complex password for the built-in Administrator account and rotating it regularly (at least every 90 days). Consider renaming the account and disabling it when not actively in use to reduce the risk of brute-force or credential-stuffing attacks targeting this well-known account.</div><a name="ae5ac015-0252-4cd1-bf7f-6c623ad58823"><h1 class="NOTOCHeading4">Duplicate Objects</h1></a><div>The following section details duplicate objects detected in the domain PHARMAX.LOCAL. These objects may indicate replication issues or administrative errors that require attention.</div><br /><div style="word-break: break-word; overflow-wrap: anywhere; "><table class="tabledefault" style="width:100%; table-layout: fixed;"><colgroup><col style="max-width:40%; min-width:40%; width:40%" /><col style="max-width:20%; min-width:20%; width:20%" /><col style="max-width:20%; min-width:20%; width:20%" /><col style="max-width:20%; min-width:20%; width:20%" /></colgroup>

<div><span style="font-weight: bold; text-decoration: underline;">Health Check:</span></div><br /><div><span style="font-weight: bold;">Corrective Actions: </span>Ensure there are no duplicate objects in Active Directory. Duplicate objects can cause various issues such as authentication problems, replication conflicts, and administrative overhead. It is recommended to regularly audit and clean up any duplicate objects to maintain a healthy and efficient Active Directory environment.</div><a name="2115cd1e-9ecd-4ca7-a6ba-fa376a3309a8"><h2 class="Heading2">2.2 ACAD.PHARMAX.LOCAL</h2></a><div>This section provides an overview of the Active Directory domain configuration, including key settings and operational details.</div><br /><div style="word-break: break-word; overflow-wrap: anywhere; "><table class="tabledefault" style="width:100%; table-layout: fixed;"><colgroup><col style="max-width:40%; min-width:40%; width:40%" /><col style="max-width:60%; min-width:60%; width:60%" /></colgroup>

<a name="e8fa3097-044c-43a2-b97f-781114b3c0c3"><h3 class="Heading3">2.2.1 FSMO Roles</h3></a><div style="word-break: break-word; overflow-wrap: anywhere; "><table class="tabledefault" style="width:100%; table-layout: fixed;"><colgroup><col style="max-width:40%; min-width:40%; width:40%" /><col style="max-width:60%; min-width:60%; width:60%" /></colgroup>
<tbody>
<tr><th>Infrastructure Master</th><td style="font-family: 'Segoe Ui'; font-size: 0.83rem; text-align: left; font-weight: normal; color: #565656; background-color: #fff4c7;">acade-dc-01v.acad.pharmax.local</td></tr>
<tr><th>PDC Emulator Name</th><td>acade-dc-01v.acad.pharmax.local</td></tr>
<tr><th>RID Master</th><td>acade-dc-01v.acad.pharmax.local</td></tr>
<tr><th>Domain Naming Master</th><td>Server-DC-01V.pharmax.local</td></tr>
<tr><th>Schema Master</th><td>Server-DC-01V.pharmax.local</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
<div class="Caption">Table 160 - FSMO Roles - acad.pharmax.local</div><br /></div>
<div><span style="font-weight: bold; text-decoration: underline;">Health Check:</span></div><br /><div><span style="font-weight: bold;">Best Practice: </span>The infrastructure master role in the domain ACAD.PHARMAX.LOCAL should be held by a domain controller that is not a global catalog server. The infrastructure master is responsible for updating references from objects in its domain to objects in other domains. If the infrastructure master runs on a global catalog server, it will not function properly because the global catalog holds a partial replica of every object in the forest, and it will not update the references. This issue does not affect forests that have a single domain.</div><br /><div><span style="font-weight: bold;">Reference: </span>http://go.microsoft.com/fwlink/?LinkId=168841</div><a name="567e8ba6-e816-4da8-8540-9537a5e27602"><h3 class="Heading3">2.2.2 Domain and Trusts</h3></a><a name="7535439c-9939-4dbd-bd17-234ceee3ff19"><h1 class="NOTOCHeading4">pharmax.local Trust Details</h1></a><div style="word-break: break-word; overflow-wrap: anywhere; "><table class="tabledefault" style="width:100%; table-layout: fixed;"><colgroup><col style="max-width:40%; min-width:40%; width:40%" /><col style="max-width:60%; min-width:60%; width:60%" /></colgroup>

<div><span style="font-weight: bold; text-decoration: underline;">Health Check:</span></div><br /><div><span style="font-weight: bold;">Best Practice: </span>Ensure that AES Kerberos encryption is enabled on all Active Directory trusts. RC4 encryption is considered weak and vulnerable to various attacks. Enabling AES encryption on trusts enhances Kerberos security and aligns with modern security standards. Reference: https://techcommunity.microsoft.com/t5/itops-talk-blog/tough-questions-answered-can-i-disable-rc4-etype-for-kerberos-on/ba-p/382718</div></div><div><br /><div class="Footer">Page 15</div></div></div></div><div class="portrait"><div class="Normal" style="padding-top: 5.92rem; padding-left: 5.92rem; padding-bottom: 5.92rem; padding-right: 5.92rem;">
<div style="min-height: 247mm" ><div><div class="Header">Microsoft Active Directory As Built Report - v1.0</div><br /></div><a name="cdc08035-a233-40cb-a58e-8011a7c5139d"><h4 class="Heading3">2.2.2.1 Domain and Trusts Diagram</h4></a><div align="Center">
<img src="data:image/png;base64, " alt="Domain and Trusts Diagram" height="600" width="558" />
</div>
</div><div><br /><div class="Footer">Page 16</div></div></div></div><div class="portrait"><div class="Normal" style="padding-top: 5.92rem; padding-left: 5.92rem; padding-bottom: 5.92rem; padding-right: 5.92rem;">

<a name="589f242a-2d60-4f11-9b1b-1d0d065c0528"><h5 class="Heading5">2.2.3.2.1 Privileged Groups (Built-in)</h5></a><div>The following section provides detailed information about the user members of each privileged group. Groups without members are excluded.</div><br /><a name="bde2c7dc-28d7-4ca1-994d-dbbedaa94e3e"><h1 class="NOTOCHeading4">Domain Admins (2 Members)</h1></a><div style="word-break: break-word; overflow-wrap: anywhere; "><table class="tabledefault" style="width:100%; table-layout: fixed;"><colgroup><col style="max-width:50%; min-width:50%; width:50%" /><col style="max-width:20%; min-width:20%; width:20%" /><col style="max-width:15%; min-width:15%; width:15%" /><col style="max-width:15%; min-width:15%; width:15%" /></colgroup>

<a name="c8f29f3c-d65a-45c4-bc0e-90782a4f45de"><h5 class="Heading5">2.2.3.2.2 Privileged Group (Non-Default)</h5></a><div>The following section provides a summary of privileged groups with the AdminCount attribute set to 1 (excluding default groups).</div><br /><div style="word-break: break-word; overflow-wrap: anywhere; "><table class="tabledefault" style="width:100%; table-layout: fixed;"><colgroup><col style="max-width:50%; min-width:50%; width:50%" /><col style="max-width:50%; min-width:50%; width:50%" /></colgroup>

<tr><td>Prueba</td><td>S-1-5-21-370360276-377477351-3184454278-1114</td></tr>
<tr><td>SCCM-GMSA</td><td>S-1-5-21-370360276-377477351-3184454278-1104</td></tr>
</tbody></table>
